pageTimings.text:
Pip noticed it on a Tuesday.
The grey fog of the Fog Marsh was closer.
It had never come this near before.
He marked the edge with a stick.

By the end of the week, the fog had crept closer.
The mushrooms at the edge were going out.
One by one.
The fog was not stopping.

Pip told Luna.
Luna listened carefully.
"Something is wrong at the marsh," she said.
"We must go in."
Pip, Fern, and Dewdrop came too.

Inside the marsh, it was colder.
The trees drooped.
The water did not move.
Dewdrop led them through the fog.
She could feel the water.

At the centre of the marsh, they found it.
A figure made of grey mist.
It sat against a dead tree.
It was very still.
It looked very sad.

Dewdrop went close.
"What is wrong?" she asked.
The spirit looked up slowly.
Its eyes were dim.
"My home," it said.
"Every year, smaller."

The spirit pointed to the edges.
They looked.
There were things left behind.
A rope.
An old lamp.
A pile of mushroom caps.
Left by Moonwood creatures.

"We didn't know," said Pip.
"We left things without thinking," said Fern.
The spirit looked at them.
"Every year, more things."
"Every year, a little smaller."

"We'll clear it," said Pip.
"Now," said Fern.
They spent the afternoon clearing the marsh edge.
As they worked, the spirit watched.
Slowly, it began to sit up.

When the edge was clear, the spirit stood.
Its shape was clearer now.
"Thank you," said the spirit.
It was not mist anymore.
It was something more solid.
Something more itself.

They marked the boundary at the Hollow Oak.
Everyone placed a stone.
"The marsh is the marsh," said Luna.
"Moonwood is Moonwood."
"And we leave space for both."

That evening, the fog stayed at the line.
The mushrooms began to come back on.
One by one.
Pip watched them light up.
The marsh was where it should be.
The forest was where it should be.
